verdicts.reason: The cited atlas.sum linear hash chain and sequential timestamped migration mechanism are real and correctly described, but this is an intentional architectural trade-off documented in AGENTS.md (decision chain), pitfalls.md (pf_0004), and enforcement/misc.md (migration-001), with explicit mitigation via the /rebase skill—not a bug to fix but a managed characteristic requiring procedural mitigation on long-running branches.

The quoted code at grouphandler.go:48-54 returns nil (success) for any unknown ce_type, which silently drops known-event-unknown-version mismatches indistinguishably from intentional rolling-deploy ignoring; this is a real failure mode where payload version bumps in producers are invisible to consumers, making notification delivery silently fail without surfacing as DLQ or error.

The quoted code at appclient.go:240 exists and definitively calls c.appService.UpdateAppStatus(context.Background(), ...) from providerError, which is invoked from context-carrying methods (DeleteWebhook, GetAccount, GetPaymentMethod) but receives no ctx parameter to thread through; this violates AGENTS.md's explicit rule against context.Background() in application code and breaks OTel span propagation and Ent transaction context for a database write reachable on every Stripe 401.

The quoted call site exists in cmd/billing-worker/main.go:86 and the architectural pattern is confirmed: cmd/server is the sole binary registering namespace handlers (Ledger, KafkaIngest, TaxCode) before initNamespace(), while cmd/billing-worker assumes the default namespace is already provisioned when it calls EnsureBusinessAccounts(). No Kubernetes or deployment ordering enforces cmd/server startup before cmd/billing-worker, creating a real cross-binary boot-order dependency that is undocumented in infrastructure.

The quoted default case (eventbus.go:141) exists and the failure mode is real, but it is an intentionally-designed trade-off for rolling-deploy safety that is already documented as pitfall pf-007 in enforcement/by-topic/misc.md with explicit code-review guards. The risk should be tracked via the existing enforcement rule, not as a separate findings.json entry.

Line 52 is the correct Tx() method implementing the TxCreator interface; it properly hijacks transactions and is not a helper bypassing the pattern. The risk class (helpers accepting *entdb.Client without TransactingRepo wrapping) is real and documented in enforcement rules, but the triggering call site cited is the transaction creation mechanism itself, not an instance of the violation.

The quoted code exists and accurately describes a real architectural pattern: hook registration via side-effects inside Wire provider functions (NewCustomerLedgerServiceHook, NewCustomerSubjectServiceHook, NewCustomerEntitlementValidatorServiceHook all call RegisterHooks/RegisterRequestValidator). The enforcement rules explicitly codify this as pitfall pf-006-wire-hook-audit, confirming that omitting these providers from a binary's wire.Build silently drops hooks with no compile error. The pattern is documented as a known vulnerability in the codebase.

The quoted code exists at cmd/billing-worker/main.go and the failure mode is real: billing-worker calls EnsureBusinessAccounts without ever registering namespace.Handler implementations, creating an undocumented boot-order dependency on cmd/server to register handlers and call CreateDefaultNamespace first; if billing-worker boots against a fresh database before cmd/server, subsystem provisioning (Kafka topics, ledger namespace setup) never fires.

The quoted call site at openmeter/customer/adapter/customer.go:59 exists and correctly applies case-insensitive ILIKE filters (via filter.ApplyToQuery with sql.FieldContainsFold) to the name column, which has only a plain btree index; this DOES trigger sequential scans as described. The issue is documented as a known architectural pitfall (pf_0009) in CLAUDE.md but remains a real performance problem. The v3 API exposes contains operators that compile to leading-wildcard ILIKE, and the customer schema lacks the pg_trgm GIN indexes required to serve this query pattern efficiently.

The quoted code exists at openmeter/server/server.go line 212-214 and does violate the context propagation rule by passing context.Background() to models.NewStatusProblem in an error handler. The failure mode is real: v1 validation-error RFC 7807 responses lose request-id and OTel span context. While this is due to kin-openapi's ErrorHandler callback signature omitting *http.Request, it remains a valid rule violation in application code that should be tracked, though fixing it may require wrapping or rewriting the v1 validation middleware.
